blocks.text: Two pointers from both ends, swap and move inward until they cross. O(n) time, O(1) space.

questions.qText: Two Sum — indices of two numbers adding to target

blocks.text: One pass with a HashMap of value to index; for each x check if target minus x was already seen. O(n) time and space.
At each index, either extend the running sum or restart from the current element; track the best. O(n) time, O(1) space.

lines: for(int i=1;i<a.length;i++){ cur=Math.max(a[i], cur+a[i]); best=Math.max(best, cur); }

blocks.text: Two pointers with leftMax/rightMax: water above each bar is min(leftMax, rightMax) minus its height. Always move the smaller side inward. O(n) time, O(1) space.

questions.qText: Check if a string is a palindrome

blocks.text: Two pointers from both ends comparing characters, optionally skipping non-alphanumerics and ignoring case. O(n).

questions.qText: Check if two strings are anagrams

blocks.text: Count character frequencies (int[26] for lowercase) and compare, or sort both strings and compare. O(n).
Sliding window: expand the right pointer; on a duplicate, move left past its last index. Track max length with a lastIndex map. O(n).

questions.qText: How does a HashMap work internally?

blocks.text: An array of buckets; index = spread(hashCode) & (capacity minus 1). Collisions form a linked list that treeifies past 8 entries (Java 8). Resizes at load factor 0.75. Average O(1) get/put.
Count frequencies in one pass (a LinkedHashMap preserves order), then return the first key with count 1. O(n).
Use the sorted string (or a 26-length count signature) as a map key and group words under it. O(n·k log k).
Walk the list re-pointing each node's next to the previous node. O(n) time, O(1) space.

lines: while(h!=null){ ListNode n=h.next; h.next=p; p=h; h=n; }

questions.qText: Detect a cycle in a linked list (Floyd's)

blocks.text: Slow moves 1, fast moves 2; if they meet there is a cycle. To find the start, reset one pointer to head and advance both by 1 until they meet. O(n) time, O(1) space.
Dummy head plus a tail pointer; repeatedly attach the smaller head and advance. O(n+m).
Push openers; on a closer, pop and verify the match. Valid iff the stack is empty at the end. O(n).

questions.qText: Implement a min-stack with O(1) getMin

blocks.text: Keep an auxiliary stack of running minimums (or store pairs of value and current-min). push/pop/top/getMin all O(1).
Keep a decreasing stack of indices; when the current value is greater, pop and record the answer. Each index is pushed/popped once, so O(n).

questions.qText: Implement a queue using two stacks

blocks.text: An 'in' stack for enqueue and an 'out' stack for dequeue; move in to out only when out is empty. Amortized O(1).
Monotonic decreasing deque of indices; the front is the window max. Pop smaller values from the back and out-of-window indices from the front. O(n).

questions.qText: What is a circular queue and why use it?

blocks.text: A fixed-size ring buffer where the tail wraps to the front, reusing freed slots and avoiding element shifting. Used in buffers, scheduling and streaming.
Preorder (root, left, right), inorder (left, root, right — sorted for a BST), postorder (left, right, root), and level-order (BFS with a queue). All O(n).
Recurse with a (min, max) range each node must satisfy, tightening as you descend; or check that an inorder traversal is strictly increasing. O(n).

questions.qText: Lowest common ancestor in a binary tree

blocks.text: Recurse; if a node equals p or q return it; if both left and right recursions return non-null, the current node is the LCA. O(n).

questions.qText: BFS vs DFS — when to use which?

blocks.text: BFS (queue) finds shortest paths in unweighted graphs and explores level by level. DFS (stack/recursion) suits path existence, connected components, topological sort and backtracking. Both O(V+E).
Scan the grid; on each unvisited land cell, increment the count and flood-fill (DFS/BFS) its connected land. O(rows times cols).

questions.qText: Detect a cycle in a directed graph

blocks.text: DFS with three colors (white/gray/black): a back-edge to a gray in-progress node means a cycle. Or use Kahn's topological sort — leftover nodes imply a cycle. O(V+E).

questions.qText: Binary search and its common variants

blocks.text: On sorted data, compare with the middle and halve the range. O(log n). Variants: first/last occurrence, lower/upper bound, and search in a rotated array.

lines: while(lo<=hi){ int m=(lo+hi)>>>1; if(a[m]==x) return m; if(a[m]<x) lo=m+1; else hi=m-1; }

questions.qText: Search in a rotated sorted array

blocks.text: Modified binary search: one half is always sorted — determine which, then check whether the target lies inside it to choose the side. O(log n).

questions.qText: Compare merge sort, quick sort, and heap sort

blocks.text: Merge sort: O(n log n) stable, O(n) space, good for linked lists/external sort. Quick sort: O(n log n) average, O(n squared) worst, in-place, cache-friendly. Heap sort: O(n log n), in-place, not stable.
Pick a pivot, partition so smaller elements go left and larger right, then recurse on both halves. Randomized or median-of-three pivots avoid the O(n squared) worst case on sorted input.

questions.qText: What is recursion and when does it overflow the stack?

blocks.text: A function calling itself on smaller input until a base case. Each call adds a stack frame; too-deep recursion or a missing base case causes a stack overflow. Tail recursion or converting to iteration helps.
Backtracking is choose, recurse, un-choose. Build a partial solution, recurse deeper, then undo to explore alternatives. Powers subsets, permutations, N-Queens and word search.

questions.qText: What makes a problem solvable greedily?

blocks.text: It has the greedy-choice property (a local optimum leads to a global optimum) and optimal substructure. Examples: activity selection, Huffman coding, interval scheduling, Dijkstra.
Sort by end time and greedily pick the earliest-finishing compatible interval; this maximizes the count. O(n log n).

questions.qText: Dijkstra's shortest path — idea and limits

blocks.text: Greedy plus a min-heap: repeatedly settle the nearest unvisited node and relax its edges. O((V+E) log V). Fails with negative edges — use Bellman-Ford there.
Repeatedly remove nodes with in-degree 0 and decrement their neighbors. Produces a valid DAG ordering; leftover nodes mean a cycle. O(V+E). Used for scheduling and dependencies.

Both cache subproblem results. Memoization is top-down recursion plus a cache (only needed states, recursion-depth risk). Tabulation is bottom-up iteration filling a table (no recursion, often space-optimizable).
dp[a] = min over coins c of dp[a-c]+1, with dp[0]=0. O(amount times coins).

lines: int[] dp=new int[amt+1]; Arrays.fill(dp, amt+1); dp[0]=0;
for(int a=1;a<=amt;a++) for(int c:coins) if(c<=a) dp[a]=Math.min(dp[a], dp[a-c]+1);

blocks.text: dp[i][w] = max(skip item i, take item i plus dp[i-1][w-wt]). Iterate weights descending for the 1D space-optimized version. O(n times W).
XOR all elements; pairs cancel (a xor a = 0), leaving the unique number. O(n) time, O(1) space.
x & (x-1) clears the lowest set bit (count bits / power-of-two check); x & -x isolates it; shifts multiply/divide by powers of two; masks enumerate subsets.
Use DENSE_RANK() over salary descending and filter rank = N; or a correlated subquery counting distinct higher salaries.

lines: SELECT salary, DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC) rnk FROM emp

questions.qText: WHERE vs HAVING; find employees with no orders

blocks.text: WHERE filters rows before grouping; HAVING filters after aggregation. A LEFT JOIN with the right key IS NULL finds non-matching rows.

lines: LEFT JOIN orders o ON o.emp_id = e.id

blocks.text: Atomicity (all-or-nothing), Consistency (constraints hold), Isolation (concurrent transactions do not corrupt each other), Durability (committed data survives crashes).
1NF: atomic columns. 2NF: no partial dependency on part of a composite key. 3NF: no transitive dependency. Denormalize for read-heavy workloads to avoid expensive joins, accepting redundancy.

questions.qText: Transaction isolation levels and their anomalies

blocks.text: READ UNCOMMITTED (dirty reads), READ COMMITTED (no dirty; non-repeatable reads), REPEATABLE READ (phantoms; MySQL default), SERIALIZABLE (full isolation, lowest concurrency).

questions.qText: How do indexes work and when do they hurt?

blocks.text: B-tree indexes give O(log n) lookups, range scans and sorted reads; composite indexes follow the leftmost-prefix rule. They cost write time and storage and help little on low-cardinality columns. Verify with EXPLAIN.

POST long to short, GET short to a 301 redirect. Keys: base62 of a unique/snowflake id, or random plus a collision check. Read-heavy, so front the DB with a Redis cache and read replicas. Discuss QPS/storage and 301 vs 302.

questions.qText: Explain the CAP theorem and consistent hashing

blocks.text: Under a network partition you choose Consistency or Availability. Consistent hashing places nodes and keys on a ring so adding/removing a node remaps only about 1/N keys; virtual nodes balance load.
SRP (one reason to change), OCP (extend, don't modify), LSP (subtypes substitutable), ISP (small interfaces), DIP (depend on abstractions). Together they yield loosely-coupled, testable code.
HashMap for O(1) lookup plus a doubly-linked list ordered by recency; move a node on access and evict from the tail when full. In Java, LinkedHashMap with accessOrder does it.

Percentages, ratio and proportion, averages, profit and loss, time-speed-distance, time and work, simple/compound interest, permutations and combinations, probability. Practice for speed and accuracy.
Number/letter series, blood relations, directions, seating arrangement, syllogisms, coding-decoding, clocks and calendars. Draw diagrams to avoid mistakes.
Clarify assumptions, think aloud, start with brute force then optimize, and use invariants or parity. Classics: water jugs, egg drop, weighing with a balance, bridge crossing.

questions.qText: Process vs thread; what is a deadlock?

blocks.text: A process has its own memory; threads share process memory but each has its own stack. Deadlock needs four conditions (mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, circular wait); break any one to prevent it.
Virtual memory maps pages to frames via a page table; a miss causes a page fault (load from disk). Too little RAM for the working set causes constant paging, called thrashing.

questions.qText: What happens when you type a URL and press Enter?

blocks.text: DNS resolves the domain, a TCP 3-way handshake opens on 443, TLS negotiates encryption, the browser sends an HTTP request, the server responds, and the browser parses the HTML, fetches assets and renders.
TCP is connection-oriented, reliable and ordered (HTTP, email). UDP is connectionless and fast with no guarantees (streaming, gaming, DNS).
Encapsulation (hide state behind methods), Inheritance (reuse via is-a), Polymorphism (one interface, many implementations), Abstraction (expose what, hide how).

questions.qText: Primary key vs unique key vs foreign key

blocks.text: Primary key: unique and not null, one per table. Unique key: unique but allows one null, many per table. Foreign key: references another table's key, enforcing referential integrity.

questions.qText: Explain the browser event loop and async JS

blocks.text: JS is single-threaded; the event loop runs the call stack, then drains microtasks (promises) before the next macrotask (timers, events). This is why promise callbacks run before setTimeout(0).

questions.qText: What is the Virtual DOM and reconciliation?

blocks.text: React builds a lightweight tree, diffs it against the previous one, and applies the minimal real-DOM updates. Keys give list items stable identity for efficient diffing.

questions.qText: What makes an API RESTful; idempotency

blocks.text: Resources via URIs, standard methods, stateless requests, correct status codes. GET/PUT/DELETE are idempotent; POST is not. Use idempotency keys for safe retries on writes.

questions.qText: Cookies vs localStorage vs sessionStorage; JWT auth

blocks.text: Cookies are sent with every request (use httpOnly + secure + sameSite for auth). localStorage/sessionStorage are JS-accessible (XSS-exposed). A JWT is a stateless signed token (header.payload.signature); keep it short-lived with a refresh token.

questions.qText: Container vs VM; what problem does Docker solve?

blocks.text: A VM virtualizes hardware (heavy, full guest OS); a container shares the host kernel and isolates processes (MBs, ms startup). Docker packages code and dependencies into a portable image, solving 'works on my machine'.

Pod (smallest unit), Deployment (replicas + rolling updates), Service (stable IP/load-balancing), Ingress (HTTP routing). ConfigMap/Secret for config, HPA for autoscaling, liveness/readiness probes for health.

Push, then build plus unit tests plus static analysis, build and tag an image, deploy to staging, run integration/smoke tests, rolling deploy to prod, and monitor error rate/latency with auto-rollback.
Logs (structured, centralized), metrics (Prometheus/Grafana dashboards and alerts), and traces (OpenTelemetry, one traceId across services to find where latency is).

questions.qText: Supervised vs unsupervised; the bias-variance tradeoff

blocks.text: Supervised learns from labeled data (classification/regression); unsupervised finds structure (clustering, PCA). High bias underfits; high variance overfits — balance with regularization, more data and the right model complexity.
More or augmented data, regularization (L1/L2, dropout), simpler models, cross-validation, and early stopping.

questions.qText: Precision vs recall vs F1 — when to optimize each

blocks.text: Precision: of predicted positives, how many are correct. Recall: of actual positives, how many were found. F1 balances both. Optimize recall for fraud/disease detection, precision when false positives are costly.

questions.qText: What is RAG and when do you use it over fine-tuning?

blocks.text: RAG retrieves relevant chunks from a vector store and injects them into the prompt so the LLM answers grounded in your data. Prefer it when knowledge changes or is private; fine-tune for consistent style or behavior.

questions.qText: Window functions every analyst should know

blocks.text: ROW_NUMBER/RANK/DENSE_RANK for ranking, running totals with SUM() OVER (ORDER BY ...), moving averages, LAG/LEAD for period-over-period, and PARTITION BY to reset per group.

questions.qText: Mean vs median vs mode; when is the median better?

blocks.text: The median resists outliers and skew (e.g., income), so it is often a better 'typical value' than the mean. The mode is the most frequent value, useful for categorical data.

questions.qText: What is a p-value and A/B testing basics?

blocks.text: A p-value is the probability of a result at least this extreme if the null hypothesis were true; below the significance level you reject the null. A/B tests need a hypothesis, adequate sample size, and no peeking.

questions.qText: How do you choose the right chart?

blocks.text: Trend over time to a line; comparison across categories to a bar; part-to-whole to a stacked bar (avoid many-slice pies); correlation to a scatter; distribution to a histogram/box. Label clearly and don't distort axes.

questions.qText: The test pyramid: unit vs integration vs e2e

blocks.text: Many fast unit tests at the base, fewer integration tests, and a few slow end-to-end tests at the top. This keeps feedback fast and the suite maintainable.

questions.qText: Black-box vs white-box; boundary value analysis

blocks.text: Black-box tests behavior via inputs/outputs; white-box uses internal structure (branch/path coverage). Boundary value analysis and equivalence partitioning focus tests at edges where bugs cluster.

questions.qText: How do you structure UI automation reliably?

blocks.text: Page Object Model to isolate selectors, stable locators (data-testid), explicit waits instead of sleeps, independent/idempotent tests, and API setup for data rather than clicking through the UI.
onCreate, onStart, onResume, then running, then onPause, onStop, onDestroy. A rotation destroys and recreates the Activity — persist state in a ViewModel or onSaveInstanceState.
A message object to start components or pass data — explicit (a named target) or implicit (an action the system resolves, e.g., open a URL).
A ViewModel survives configuration changes and holds UI state off the Activity; LiveData/StateFlow expose observable state the UI reacts to. MVVM separates UI from logic and improves testability.

questions.qText: Pointers, and stack vs heap in C

blocks.text: A pointer stores an address; dereference with the star operator. Locals live on the stack (auto-freed); malloc/calloc allocate on the heap and you must free them or leak. Dangling pointers and double-free are common bugs.
malloc leaves memory uninitialized; calloc zero-initializes; realloc resizes an existing block (may move it). Always check for NULL.
RAII ties resource lifetime to object scope (released in the destructor). unique_ptr is sole ownership, shared_ptr is ref-counted, weak_ptr breaks cycles. Prefer them over raw new/delete.

questions.qText: The STL containers you use most

blocks.text: vector, map/unordered_map, set/unordered_set, stack, queue, priority_queue. Know complexity: unordered containers average O(1), tree-based ordered ones O(log n).

questions.qText: JDK vs JRE vs JVM; why platform independent?

blocks.text: JVM runs bytecode; JRE is JVM plus libraries; JDK is JRE plus tools. Java compiles to bytecode (not machine code), so any JVM runs it — write once, run anywhere.
List/Set/Map via ArrayList, HashMap and friends. Checked exceptions are compiler-enforced (recoverable); unchecked extend RuntimeException. Prefer try-with-resources for cleanup.

questions.qText: What is the GIL and its impact?

blocks.text: The Global Interpreter Lock lets only one thread execute Python bytecode at a time, so threads do not parallelize CPU work (use multiprocessing) but do help I/O-bound tasks.
Generators (yield) are lazy iterators saving memory; decorators wrap functions to add behavior; comprehensions build lists/dicts/sets concisely.
A closure remembers its creation scope. var is hoisted and function-scoped; let/const are block-scoped with a temporal dead zone. this depends on call-site; arrow functions capture it lexically.
async/await is sugar over promises with try/catch. Prefer === (no coercion) over ==. Run independent async work in parallel with Promise.all.

interface supports declaration merging and object contracts; type supports unions, intersections and mapped/conditional types. Generics keep functions type-safe across many types while preserving inference.
Partial, Required, Pick, Omit, Record, ReturnType, Parameters — derive types from a single source of truth instead of duplicating.
== does type juggling (surprising results); === compares value and type. Use prepared statements (PDO) to prevent SQL injection, and know the superglobals ($_GET, $_POST, $_SESSION).

questions.qText: Vectors, data frames, and the apply family

blocks.text: R is vectorized — operate on whole vectors, not loops. Data frames hold tabular data; apply/sapply/lapply and the tidyverse (dplyr, ggplot2) drive analysis and plotting.
Blocks are anonymous chunks passed to methods (each, map). Procs and lambdas are storable block objects; lambdas check argument count and return locally, procs do not.

questions.qText: What is CUDA and the host/device model?

blocks.text: CUDA runs massively parallel kernels on the GPU (device) launched from the CPU (host). Threads group into blocks and grids; you manage host-device memory transfers and aim for high occupancy and coalesced memory access.
